Short answer: Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service — it finds, compresses and returns only the most relevant documentation (not an LLM answer) so clients can get accurate LLM responses within token limits.

Key points:
- 90% context reduction (e.g., shrink 500K tokens to ~5K highly relevant tokens)
- Token budgeting: fits optimized context to the client’s max_tokens
- LLM-agnostic: clients use their own LLM (Claude, GPT, Gemini, local models)
- No LLM inference costs for Snipara — only context-search compute
- Session context persistence and compaction across conversations
- Zero setup hosted MCP endpoint and team collaboration features

In short: Snipara enables better, cheaper, and scalable LLM usage by delivering compact, highly relevant context for any client LLM.

Short answer

Key benefits (why it matters)
- 90% context reduction — e.g., from ~500K tokens down to ~5K highly relevant tokens.  
- Token budgeting — clients specify max_tokens and Snipara fits the best content to that budget.  
- LLM‑agnostic — works with any LLM (Claude, GPT, Gemini, local models) because Snipara returns optimized context, not LLM outputs.  
- No LLM inference costs — customers run their own LLMs; Snipara charges only for context optimization.  
- Metadata & persistence — returns file/line/relevance metadata, supports session context persistence and team collaboration.  

One‑line summary
- Snipara enables any LLM to be effective and cost‑efficient by delivering a small, highly relevant, token‑budgeted context package from large documentation sets.

Short answer
- Snipara enforces a token budget for context returned to LLMs by counting tokens with tiktoken (cl100k_base), ranking candidate sections by relevance, then greedily selecting highest‑relevance content until the token budget is used. If a section only partially fits, it is smart‑truncated at a sentence boundary and marked truncated. Project docs are prioritized and budget is allocated across shared categories (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE) according to configured allocations.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Query & search mode
   - User calls rlm_context_query (params: query, max_tokens, search_mode = keyword|semantic|hybrid, include_metadata, …).
   - Engine runs a keyword and/or semantic search (or fuses both for hybrid).

2. Token counting
   - Every candidate section’s token count is measured using tiktoken with the cl100k_base encoder (tiktoken>=0.5.0).
   - These token counts are used to decide what can fit within max_tokens.

3. Budget allocation & priorities
   - There are category allocations and priorities for shared collections. Two examples in the codebase:
     - Token Budget Algorithm (shared categories): MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% (with MANDATORY highest priority).
     - Web service default allocation: project 40% (project docs get priority), MANDATORY 25%, BEST_PRACTICES 20%, GUIDELINES 10%, REFERENCE 5%.
   - Project docs are typically allocated first, then shared docs are allocated by category according to configured percentages.

4. Greedy selection
   - Candidates are ranked by relevance score.
   - The engine iterates highest→lowest relevance and:
     - If the full section fits, include it (add metadata: file, line range, relevance_score, token_count).
     - If not and some budget remains, smart_truncate the section to the remaining tokens and include that portion (mark the section truncated).
     - Stop when the token budget is exhausted or no more sections fit.

5. Smart truncation rules
   - Truncate so content fits remaining tokens (token-aware truncation using encoder).
   - Prefer cutting at sentence boundaries (., !, ?). If a perfect boundary isn’t available, it will still fit to token limit and append an ellipsis ("...").
   - Truncated sections are flagged with truncated: true in metadata; overall response has truncated: true if budget was hit.

6. Session context and system injection
   - Session context (recent conversation/context) can be included automatically; it is limited (e.g., up to ~20% of the token budget).
   - System instructions can be auto‑injected where needed.

7. Response & metadata
   - The response includes an array of sections with fields like title, content, file path, lines [start,end], relevance_score (rounded), token_count, truncated (if applicable).
   - Full response fields: sections, total_tokens used, truncated (bool), session_context (if present), suggestions (related docs), and usage/telemetry (search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache_hit).
   - Example section entry:
     {
       "title": "Authentication Flow",
       "content": "The authentication system uses JWT...",
       "file": "docs/auth.md",
       "lines": [45,120],
       "relevance_score": 0.94,
       "token_count": 1200,
       "truncated": false
     }

8. Other behaviors & limits
   - There are hard limits and enforcement functions (examples: MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION, MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD).
   - Multi‑query and decomposition tools distribute budgets across sub‑queries (default equal split unless specified).
   - The codebase provides utilities for budget allocation, per‑category allocation, and allocation‑within‑budget sorting by relevance.

Why this design
- Accurate token counting avoids surprises and keeps LLM prompts within model limits.
- Prioritized, relevance‑based greedy selection gives the best coverage of the user’s query within the budget.
- Smart truncation preserves readability (cuts at sentence boundaries) and signals consumers that content was truncated.
- Category/project allocations ensure critical project docs and mandatory rules receive higher priority.

If you want
- I can walk through a concrete example (give me a query + max_tokens) and show which sections would be selected and how many tokens each uses.
- Or I can show the pseudo‑code / key functions (smart_truncate, allocateTokenBudget) from the implementation.

Summary — how Snipara’s token budgeting works

At a high level Snipara treats the available token budget as a scarce resource and automatically selects, slices, and returns the most relevant document context that fits within that budget. The system uses exact token counting (not character/word heuristics), priority-based allocation across document categories/sources, and a greedy selection algorithm with smart truncation when only part of a section can fit.

Key components

- Exact token counting
  - Uses tiktoken with the cl100k_base encoder (GPT‑4/Claude compatible) to count tokens for every candidate section and for session/system context.
- Entry point / tool
  - Primary tool: rlm_context_query(query, max_tokens=4000, search_mode="keyword|semantic|hybrid", include_metadata=True, ...)
  - Returns sections plus metadata (file, line ranges, relevance_score, token_count), total_tokens, truncated flag, session_context, suggestions, etc.
- Search + ranking
  - Candidate sections are retrieved by keyword, semantic, or hybrid search.
  - Candidates are scored and ranked by relevance (score used for selection).
- Greedy selection within the budget
  - Token-count each candidate (using cl100k_base).
  - Iterate candidates in descending relevance and include whole sections while total_tokens + section_tokens <= max_tokens.
  - Stop when adding the next full section would exceed the budget.
- Partial inclusion with smart_truncate
  - If a section is partially includable (some remaining tokens remain but not enough for the full section) the engine calls smart_truncate().
  - smart_truncate fits the remaining tokens and truncates at a sentence boundary (. ! ?) so it doesn’t cut mid-sentence.
  - Appends "..." and marks that section’s metadata with truncated: true and sets the section token_count to the number of tokens returned.
- Session & system context
  - The engine may inject session context (previous context) and system instructions; session context is allowed but bounded (notes mention up to ~20% of the overall budget).
- Category & source budgeting (allocation)
  - The system supports budget allocation across sources/categories (project vs shared collections and shared categories such as MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE).
  - Defaults are configurable; example allocations in the docs:
    - CATEGORY_BUDGETS example: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
    - A project-first allocation example: project 40%, MANDATORY 25%, BEST_PRACTICES 20%, GUIDELINES 10%, REFERENCE 5%.
  - Allocation logic groups docs by category/source, computes per-category budgets, and then allocates tokens within each bucket (again by relevance) — but the core context_query tool also supports a simpler greedy top-relevance selection across all candidates.
- Metadata & response
  - Each returned section includes:
    - title, content, file path, lines [start,end], relevance_score (rounded), token_count
    - optional truncated flag per section if cut
  - Overall response includes:
    - sections list, total_tokens used, truncated (true if budget was filled/truncated), session_context (if any), suggestions (e.g., other docs to check)
  - Tool-level telemetry: search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache_hit, etc.
- Smart limits & enforcement
  - There are higher-level limits (collections per project, max tokens per collection, min relevance thresholds) used by allocation/enforcement code to avoid exceeding hard limits.
- Extensibility
  - Token budgets and allocation are configurable per-call (budget_allocation / custom allocation) and the engine supports multi-query decomposition and parallel queries with per-query budgets.

Typical flow (simplified)

1. Client calls rlm_context_query(query, max_tokens=4000, search_mode="hybrid").
2. Engine retrieves candidate sections (keyword/semantic/hybrid).
3. Encoder = cl100k_base; token_count each section.
4. Sort candidates by relevance.
5. Greedily append whole sections until adding another would exceed max_tokens.
6. If partial fit possible, smart_truncate the section to remaining tokens (cut at sentence boundary and append "…").
7. Inject session/system context (bounded portion of budget) if applicable.
8. Return sections + metadata + totals + suggestions.

Why this design
- Accurate counting avoids surprises from character-based heuristics.
- Greedy-by-relevance gives a fast, deterministic selection that maximizes relevance under a hard token limit.
- Category/source allocations let teams prioritize project-specific docs and important shared rules (e.g., mandatory policies).
- Smart truncation keeps returned snippets readable (no mid-sentence cuts).

If you want, I can:
- Show a small example response for a sample query.
- Explain how to tune allocation percentages for your project.
- Walk through the smart_truncate algorithm in pseudocode. Which would be most helpful?

Short answer
- Snipara enforces a hard token budget when assembling context for an LLM call: it counts tokens (using tiktoken / cl100k_base), prioritizes the most relevant sections, greedily includes them until the budget is reached, and — if needed — smart-truncates a section at a sentence boundary so the budget is exactly respected.

How it works — step by step
1. Inputs and budget
   - You call the primary tool rlm_context_query with parameters such as:
     - query (your question)
     - max_tokens (token budget, default 4000)
     - search_mode (keyword | semantic | hybrid)
     - include_metadata, session_id, etc.
   - The engine can also merge project docs with shared/team collections and apply configurable allocation rules.

2. Token counting
   - Snipara uses tiktoken with the cl100k_base encoding (GPT‑4 / Claude compatible) to count tokens for each candidate section and for session/system context.
   - Token counts are used everywhere: per-section, totals, and budgeting decisions.

3. Budget allocation & priorities
   - Shared documents are categorized with priority tiers. A common/default allocation is:
     - MANDATORY: 40%
     - BEST_PRACTICES: 30%
     - GUIDELINES: 20%
     - REFERENCE: 10%
   - When merging project + shared context, project-specific docs typically get an explicit allocation (e.g., ~40% of the full budget) and shared collections get the remainder. These percentages are configurable.
   - Categories also have priority ordering (MANDATORY > BEST_PRACTICES > GUIDELINES > REFERENCE) so higher-priority docs are considered first.

4. Candidate retrieval & ranking
   - The tool searches (keyword / semantic / hybrid) to produce candidate sections and assigns relevance scores.
   - Candidates are ranked by relevance (and internal priority) before selection.

5. Greedy selection within the token budget
   - The engine iterates the ranked candidate sections and:
     - Counts tokens for the full section.
     - If the full section fits, it’s included and its token_count is added to the running total.
     - If the full section would exceed the remaining budget but there is some remaining budget, smart_truncate() is used to take a partial section that fits exactly.
     - Stop when budget is exhausted (or no more useful candidates).
   - This is a greedy highest-relevance-first selection.

6. Smart truncation behavior
   - If only part of a section fits, smart_truncate:
     - Truncates to fit the remaining tokens using the same tiktoken encoder.
     - Cuts at a sentence boundary (preferably ".", "!" or "?") so you don’t cut mid-sentence.
     - Appends an ellipsis ("...") marker to the truncated content.
     - Marks that section as truncated in the metadata (truncated: true).
   - The response-level truncated flag is set if the engine hit the budget limit.

7. Session & system context
   - Session context and/or system instructions can be auto-injected and count toward the token budget (implementation notes: session context inclusion is capped — e.g., up to ~20% of the budget in some flows). System instructions may also be injected automatically to guide retrieval and formatting.

8. Response & metadata
   - rlm_context_query returns:
     - sections: list of selected sections with metadata (title, content, file path, line range, relevance_score, token_count, truncated flag if applicable)
     - total_tokens (tokens used)
     - truncated (whether results hit the max_tokens budget)
     - session_context (if included)
     - suggestions (e.g., related docs to check)
   - Additional operational metadata (search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache_hit) is included in tool usage/telemetry.

Why this design
- Predictable cost and LLM prompt size: counting tokens and enforcing budgets prevents LLM inputs from exceeding limits or surprise cost.
- Relevance-first: greedy selection ensures you get the most relevant information within the available budget.
- Human-readable truncation: smart_truncate keeps excerpts readable by cutting at sentence boundaries and annotating truncation.
- Configurable allocations: project vs shared and per-category budgets can be tuned for different teams/workflows.

Example (conceptual)
- Call: rlm_context_query(query="How does authentication work?", max_tokens=4000, search_mode="hybrid")
- Engine finds many candidate sections, ranks them, counts tokens (cl100k_base), picks the top sections until ~4000 tokens are used, uses smart_truncate on the last section if necessary, and returns a sections array with file, lines, relevance_score, token_count, and truncated flags plus total_tokens and suggestions.

If you want
- I can show a small example calculation demonstrating token counts with cl100k_base, or walk through how to change the allocation percentages for your project/team. Which would you prefer?

Short answer
- Shared context collections are team-level collections of documents (coding standards, security rules, templates, best practices, etc.) that are stored once and automatically injected into queries for any project that links to the collection. They let a team ensure every developer and every AI-assisted session follows the same non‑negotiable rules and recommended patterns.

How they work (overview)
- Create a shared collection (team admin/owner). Upload documents into it (policy, coding standards, templates) and tag each document with a category and priority.
- Link the collection to one or more projects (done in the dashboard). Once linked, any rlm_context_query or RLM session for those projects will automatically include the shared context.

Summary — how it works (step‑by‑step)

1) CLI requests a device code
- Client (CLI) POSTs to /api/oauth/device/code with JSON: { "client_id": "snipara_cli" } (optionally "auto_provision": true).
- Response includes:
  - device_code (opaque string the server gives the client)
  - user_code (human readable code, e.g. "ABCD-1234")
  - verification_uri (URL the user should open)
  - verification_uri_complete (URL that includes the user_code)
  - expires_in (seconds until the device_code/user_code expire)
  - interval (recommended polling interval in seconds)

Example response:
{
  "device_code":"abc123...",
  "user_code":"ABCD-1234",
  "verification_uri":"https://snipara.com/device",
  "verification_uri_complete":"https://snipara.com/device?code=ABCD-1234",
  "expires_in":900,
  "interval":5
}

2) User authorizes in the browser
- The CLI shows the verification_uri and user_code (or offers verification_uri_complete).
- User opens the URL and enters the user_code (or visits the complete URL) and signs in/consents on the web page (GET /api/oauth/device/authorize shows the authorize UI).

3) CLI polls for the access token
- The CLI repeatedly POSTs to /api/oauth/device/token with:
  {
    "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
    "device_code": "<device_code>",
    "client_id": "snipara_cli"
  }
- While the user has not approved, the server returns an error like:
  { "error": "authorization_pending" }
- The CLI should poll at the server-provided interval until success or until expires_in is reached.

4) On success the server returns tokens
- Successful response contains:
  {
    "access_token": "snipara_at_...",
    "token_type": "Bearer",
    "expires_in": <seconds>,
    "refresh_token": "snipara_rt_..."
  }
- Access tokens use the prefix snipara_at_ (server validates tokens via DB/expiry checks).

5) Client stores/uses the token
- Typical clients store the token locally (Snipara client uses ~/.snipara/tokens.json).
- Subsequent MCP requests use Authorization: Bearer <access_token>.
- The server also supports token refresh (/api/oauth/token) and revoke (/api/oauth/revoke). There are endpoints to list and delete stored tokens (e.g., /api/settings/tokens).

Behavior in the MCP client and authentication precedence
- When running an MCP client, authentication priority is:
  1. Use stored OAuth token from ~/.snipara/tokens.json (if present and valid).
  2. Fall back to X-API-Key from environment (project or team API key).
- If you want to bypass OAuth and force API key auth, set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the client environment (recommended), or remove ~/.snipara/tokens.json.

Notes / recommendations
- Follow the server-provided "interval" when polling; respect expires_in to stop polling after expiration.
- The device flow follows RFC 8628 semantics (device_code/user_code, polling model).
- Use auto_provision if you want the server to create account/project automatically when the user authorizes (available as an option on the /device/code request).
- For troubleshooting: if you see "authorization_pending" keep polling; if you need to cancel or revoke, use the revoke endpoints.

Quick cURL examples

1) Request device code
curl -X POST https://snipara.com/api/oauth/device/code \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"client_id":"snipara_cli","auto_provision":true}'

2) Poll for token
curl -X POST https://snipara.com/api/oauth/device/token \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"grant_type":"urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code","device_code":"abc123...","client_id":"snipara_cli"}'

That covers the end-to-end device flow behavior in Snipara and how the client should handle it. If you want, I can show a small CLI pseudocode example that implements the polling and storage.

Summary — what it is
- Snipara implements the standard OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization (Device Flow, RFC 8628) so CLI and other non-browser clients can authenticate without copying API keys.
- Typical user story: run a CLI command, get a short user code + verification URL, open the URL in a browser, enter the code and sign in/approve. The CLI polls the server until the user authorizes and an access token is returned.

1. Client requests a device code
   - POST /api/oauth/device/code
   - Typical request body:
     { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }
   - Optional: auto_provision: true to auto-create account/project if needed.
   - Server response contains:
     { 
       "device_code": "...",            // long opaque code client must use to poll
       "user_code": "ABCD-1234",        // short code user enters in browser
       "verification_uri": "https://snipara.com/device",
       "verification_uri_complete": "https://snipara.com/device?code=ABCD-1234",
       "expires_in": 900,               // seconds until device_code expires
       "interval": 5                    // suggested poll interval (seconds)
     }

2. User authorizes in a browser
   - User opens verification_uri (or uses verification_uri_complete which pre-fills the code).
   - The authorize page shows fields to enter the user_code (or uses the complete link) and signs the user into Snipara, approves the device.

3. Client polls for tokens
   - Client repeatedly POSTs to /api/oauth/device/token:
     {
       "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
       "device_code": "abc123...",
       "client_id": "snipara_cli"
     }
   - While waiting, server responds with:
     { "error": "authorization_pending" }
   - After user approves, server responds with a successful token set:
     {
       "access_token": "snipara_at_...",
       "token_type": "Bearer",
       "expires_in": 2592000,
       "refresh_token": "snipara_rt_..."
     }

4. Use tokens for requests
   - Access tokens are sent in the Authorization header:
     Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
   - Typical server token format has the snipara_at_ prefix; token validation checks DB/expiry and scopes.

5. Token lifecycle operations
   - Refresh: /api/oauth/token (standard refresh flow) to get new access_token via refresh_token.
   - Revoke: POST /api/oauth/revoke to revoke access/refresh tokens.
   - The server exposes endpoints for listing/revoking tokens in user settings.

- Device flow is used by the MCP client for secure authentication without manual API key copying.
- The client commonly uses client_id "snipara_cli".
- The server returns interval and expires_in — clients should honor interval to avoid excessive polling and stop after expires_in.
- The MCP authentication priority is: check for a stored OAuth token (e.g., ~/.snipara/tokens.json) first, then fall back to API key environment variables.
- For debugging or to force API-key usage, you can set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the client env or remove ~/.snipara/tokens.json.

Summary
- Snipara implements the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628) so CLI/desktop clients can authenticate without copying API keys.
- The flow: client requests a device code → user visits a verification URL and enters a user code (or follows a complete link) → client polls the token endpoint until the user authorizes → server returns an access token (and refresh token).
- Tokens are stored locally (the client looks in ~/.snipara/tokens.json). If no OAuth token is available the client falls back to an API key (X-API-Key) unless you force API-key-only mode with SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true.

Step-by-step (with endpoint details)
1) Request a device code
- Endpoint: POST /api/oauth/device/code
- Request body example:
  { "client_id": "snipara_cli", "auto_provision": true }
  (client_id is optional; auto_provision can ask the server to auto-create an account/project)
- Response fields:
  - device_code: short opaque code the client will poll with
  - user_code: code the user types into the web page (e.g. "ABCD-1234")
  - verification_uri: URL where the user authenticates (e.g. https://snipara.com/device)
  - verification_uri_complete: URL that includes the user_code (one click)
  - expires_in: how long the device_code/user_code are valid (e.g. 900 seconds)
  - interval: recommended polling interval in seconds (e.g. 5)

Example:
curl -X POST https://snipara.com/api/oauth/device/code -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"client_id":"snipara_cli"}'

2) User authorizes in the browser
- User opens verification_uri (or verification_uri_complete) and enters the user_code (or just follows the complete link).
- Server may use GET /api/oauth/device/authorize?code=ABCD-1234 to render the authorization page.

3) Client polls for an access token
- Endpoint: POST /api/oauth/device/token
- Request body:
  {
    "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
    "device_code": "abc123...",
    "client_id": "snipara_cli"
  }
- Responses:
  - While user hasn’t authorized yet: { "error": "authorization_pending" } (client should continue polling using the recommended interval)
  - On success:
    {
      "access_token": "snipara_at_...",
      "token_type": "Bearer",
      "expires_in": 2592000,
      "refresh_token": "snipara_rt_..."
    }
  - access_token is used in requests as: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...

Client behavior and token usage
- The MCP client stores/reads OAuth tokens (e.g. ~/.snipara/tokens.json). When present, the client will prefer OAuth (Authorization: Bearer).
- If no OAuth token is available, the client falls back to API key auth via X-API-Key: rlm_...
- You can force API-key-only operation by setting SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the client environment (useful for testing or CI).

Practical notes / defaults
- Polling interval default: 5s (server may return a different interval).
- Device code expiry example: 900s (15 minutes) — start the browser step before this expires.
- Successful token response includes a refresh_token and expires_in (e.g. 30 days). If tokens expire or are revoked, re-run the device flow.
- auto_provision=true option allows creating an account/project automatically during verification if supported.

Troubleshooting
- If you get "authorization_pending", wait and keep polling at the recommended interval.
- If OAuth is expired or you want to bypass OAuth, either re-run the device flow or remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json to force API-key auth (or set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH).
- If you need to test MCP endpoints with an API key, use the X-API-Key header:
  curl -X POST "https://api.snipara.com/mcp/snipara" -H "X-API-Key: rlm_your_key" -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/list"}'

References
- Implements RFC 8628 device flow; endpoints: POST /api/oauth/device/code, GET /api/oauth/device/authorize, POST /api/oauth/device/token.
- Token storage and priority: check ~/.snipara/tokens.json (OAuth) → fall back to env/API key unless SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH is set.

Snipara uses a strict layered architecture (a layered / “clean” / onion-style pattern) with a clear dependency direction: outer layers depend on inner layers only, inner layers never import outer layers.

Key layers (top → bottom)
- Routes (apps/web/src/app/api/) — thin HTTP handlers that must: authenticate (requireAuth), validate input (Zod), delegate to a service or repository, and return a response.
- lib/jobs/ — orchestrators / processors (can be called by routes).
- lib/services/ — external API adapters (lazy initialization, retries; e.g., Stripe client).
- lib/db/queries/ — repository layer (all DB access lives here; route handlers MUST NOT call Prisma directly).
- types/ and utils/ — core/shared code with NO dependencies on other layers.

Rules enforced
- Lower layers cannot import from higher layers.
- types/ and utils/ must import nothing.
- All DB queries go through lib/db/queries and must include permission checks.
- Route handlers are intentionally thin and delegate business logic to services/queries.

In short: a strict layered/clean architecture with well‑defined layers and one‑way dependency flow (Routes → Jobs → Services → Repositories → Types/Utils).

Short answer
- Snipara (RLM) reduces the amount of source material you send to the LLM by selecting only the relevant sections and enforcing a token budget. For a 500K-token codebase it can often reduce the query payload to ~5K tokens (docs cite this example), which fits easily inside a 128K LLM window and avoids sending the whole corpus.
- That reduction directly cuts LLM input-token costs (and usually latency, failure rate, and engineering complexity). Typical savings are in the 90–99% range depending on query and configuration.

How Snipara helps (mechanisms)
1. Context optimization (rlm_context_query)
   - Performs keyword/semantic/hybrid search and ranks candidate sections by relevance.
   - Greedy token-budget selection: picks highest-relevance sections until your max_tokens budget is used.
   - Smart truncation: if a section partially fits, it truncates at sentence boundaries and marks it truncated.

2. Token budgeting and metadata
   - You set max_tokens for the returned context (so the LLM receives only what you budget).
   - Uses tiktoken (cl100k_base) to count tokens precisely.
   - Returns metadata (file, lines, relevance_score, token_count) so you can audit what was included.

3. Shared context + session context
   - Shared team-level docs (MANDATORY / BEST_PRACTICES / GUIDELINES / REFERENCE) can be injected first with a configurable budget percent (docs default to querying shared context first, e.g., ~30%).
   - Session context can persist across compaction (Snipara may reserve up to ~20% of budget for session context).

4. Recursive decomposition (near-infinite context)
   - For very large / complex questions, rlm_decompose + rlm_multi_query breaks a question into sub-queries and executes them in sequence/parallel so you never need to shove the entire 500K into one call.

5. No extra LLM inference costs from Snipara
   - Snipara prepares and returns the optimized context; your client uses your own LLM for inference. Snipara itself doesn’t run the LLM inference (so there’s no extra inference billing on Snipara’s side).

Concrete cost-savings examples (formula + scenarios)
Use this formula:
- Savings per request = (tokens_before - tokens_after)/1000 * price_per_1k
- Percent token reduction = 1 - tokens_after / tokens_before

Example assumptions:
- LLM charges price_per_1k for input tokens (you can substitute your model’s price).
- tokens_before: what you would otherwise send (we compare vs 128K window here).
- tokens_after: Snipara-optimized context (use 5K as the example).

1) Example price = $0.03 per 1K input tokens (illustrative)
- Without Snipara (sending 128K): cost = 128 * $0.03 = $3.84
- With Snipara (5K): cost = 5 * $0.03 = $0.15
- Savings per request = $3.84 - $0.15 = $3.69
- Percent reduction ≈ 1 - 5/128 = 96.1% token reduction
- If you make 10,000 such queries/month → monthly savings = $3.69 * 10,000 = $36,900

2) More/less expensive models
- If price_per_1k = $0.06, savings per request ≈ $7.38
- If price_per_1k = $0.01, savings per request ≈ $1.23

(You can plug your model’s exact per-1k token price into the formula.)

Additional non-monetary savings
- Lower latency: smaller inputs → faster responses.
- Fewer LLM failures/overflows: you reliably fit within the window.
- Less engineering complexity: fewer ad-hoc chunking, fewer post-aggregation steps.
- Fewer follow-up queries (often): higher-quality, focused contexts mean fewer clarifying LLM calls.

Plan & operational cost notes
- Snipara’s docs mention a context-query plan/limits (FREE/PRO/TEAM/ENTERPRISE) for query volume; pick the plan that fits your query volume. Those plan fees are small relative to LLM inference savings for medium-to-high usage.
- Snipara does not eliminate LLM costs entirely — it minimizes how many tokens you send to the LLM and provides tooling (decompose / multi-query) to handle what can’t fit in one pass.

Recommendations (practical next steps)
1. Start by running rlm_context_query with a conservative max_tokens that fits comfortably inside your 128K including prompt + response (e.g., max_tokens = 20,000 or 40,000 depending on output needs).
2. Use hybrid search mode (keyword + semantic) and include shared context first if you want team rules prioritized.
3. For very large/complex questions use rlm_decompose + rlm_multi_query to orchestrate sub-queries instead of sending huge chunks.
4. Estimate your per-query LLM cost, plug into the formula above, and multiply by expected monthly queries to quantify expected savings.
5. If you expect many queries/month, choose an appropriate Snipara plan (PRO/TEAM) so your query volume is supported.

- Snipara (RLM) reduces a giant codebase (500K tokens) down to a small, highly relevant context payload (typical claim: ~5K tokens) that fits easily inside a 128K LLM context window.

How Snipara actually helps (mechanics)
1. Index + hybrid search: it indexes your 500K tokens and finds the most relevant sections (keyword + semantic / hybrid modes).
2. Token-budgeted selection: you tell Snipara max_tokens (e.g., 4–8K) and it greedily selects highest-relevance sections that fit that budget using tiktoken to count tokens precisely.

5. Session & recursion: session context persists across queries, and recursive tools (rlm_decompose / rlm_multi_query) let you break large questions into efficient sub-queries so you never need to stream the whole 500K into the model.
6. LLM-agnostic / zero LLM costs from Snipara: Snipara doesn’t run the LLM for you — it only prepares the context. You pay your LLM provider for inference, but on far fewer tokens.

Why this matters for a 128K window
- 500K tokens > 128K window, so naïvely you must either (a) truncate lots of docs and risk missing important info, or (b) run multiple model calls covering different chunks (more calls, more tokens, higher cost and latency).  
- Snipara produces a compact, high-quality context (e.g., ~5K tokens) that fits comfortably inside your 128K window along with system prompts and expected output tokens. That avoids multiple calls and keeps the answer grounded.

  - Typical follow-up: run rlm_multi_query (or repeated rlm_context_query calls) on the produced sub-queries, then synthesize the results.

4. Want to control token budgeting, parallelism, or execution order → rlm_decompose + rlm_multi_query.

Short answer — pick the search mode based on whether you need exact matching (keyword), conceptual/architecture understanding (semantic), or a mix (hybrid):

- Keyword
  - When: looking for a specific function, exact string, error code, API endpoint, or a code snippet.
  - Why: fast, deterministic term-matching; returns exact hits you can open and read.
  - Example: "find function sendEmail", "HTTP 502 error text", "route /api/projects".
  - Tip: Use when you already have good keywords or file names.

- Semantic
  - When: "How does X work?" questions, architecture or design explanations, concept searches, or when you don’t know the exact terms.
  - Why: embedding similarity finds conceptually relevant sections even if wording differs.
  - Example: "how does authentication work?", "explain ingestion pipeline", "how do we handle multi-tenant data?"
  - Tip: Better for high-level understanding and discovery.

- Hybrid
  - When: general documentation searches, best-practices, or mixed queries where you want both relevant concepts and precise matches.
  - Why: combines keyword precision with semantic breadth — good default for broad/ambiguous searches.
  - Example: "best practices for database migrations", "how to deploy with docker compose".
  - Tip: Use when you want the best overall results and aren’t sure which mode fits.

Other practical notes
- Cost/plan: semantic and hybrid use embeddings (Pro+); keyword is the cheapest/freest.
- Performance: keyword is fastest; semantic/hybrid may take slightly longer.
- Workflow guidance: Always try rlm_context_query first (choose the mode there). If rlm_context_query errors (500/timeout), fall back to local file read/grep for known paths.
- Quick decision flow: Need exact text → keyword. Need conceptual/architecture → semantic. Unsure or mixed needs → hybrid.

Recommended mapping (quick reference)
- Looking for a specific function or exact text (e.g., "find function parseDate" / error code): keyword — fast, precise.
- Error code or exact log message lookup: keyword — matches tokens/text exactly.
- API endpoint discovery (exact route, param names): keyword.
- "How does X work?" / conceptual questions (authentication flow, caching strategy): semantic — finds relevant sections even if wording differs.
- Architecture understanding (system overviews, design decisions): semantic.
- General documentation search, best practices, or when you’re not sure which terms to use: hybrid — combines keyword precision with semantic breadth.
- Best-practices or “show me examples of X” searches: hybrid.

Examples
- Keyword: "GET /api/users", "TypeError: undefined is not a function", "createUser.ts"
- Semantic: "how authentication works", "rate limiting design", "why use pgvector"
- Hybrid: "deploying snipara on docker", "react component testing best practices"

Practical tips
- Start with keyword for exact symbols, filenames, or error strings. If results are sparse or you don’t know exact wording, switch to semantic.
- Use hybrid when you want both exact matches and related conceptual results in one query.
- If you need pattern/regex matching across docs, use rlm_search (regex) in addition to keyword/semantic queries.
- Be aware: semantic and hybrid (embedding-based features) are gated to Pro+ plans; keyword is available on free tier.
- If the Snipara MCP API fails (500/timeout), fall back to local file read/grep per the workflow.

If you want, tell me a few example queries you typically run and I’ll recommend which mode to use for each and give a one-line query you can paste into rlm_context_query.

Short answer — pick the mode that matches what you’re trying to find:

- Keyword
  - Use when: looking for an exact term, function name, error code, or specific API endpoint.
  - Pros: very fast, deterministic, low cost (Free plan), precise for literal matches.
  - Cons: misses conceptually relevant results that don’t contain the exact words.
  - Example: "getUserById", "ERROR 503", "POST /api/projects"

- Semantic
  - Use when: you want conceptual or explanatory matches (how something works, architecture, design rationale).
  - Pros: finds relevant content even if wording differs; great for “How does X work?” and architecture/why questions.
  - Cons: slower and more resource‑intensive; requires embeddings (Pro+).
  - Example: "how authentication works", "database sharding strategy"

- Hybrid
  - Use when: you want the best of both — general documentation search, best practices, or when you need conceptual relevance plus literal matches.
  - Pros: highest-quality results for broad searches; balances precision and recall.
  - Cons: requires Pro+; more compute than keyword.
  - Example: "best practices for API versioning", broad searches across docs

Quick guideline table (practical):
- Exact function, error, or endpoint → Keyword
- "How does X work?" or architecture → Semantic
- General documentation, best practices, or mixed needs → Hybrid

Other tips
- Start with hybrid for broad/uncertain queries; switch to keyword when you know exact identifiers.
- If Snipara returns errors/timeouts, fall back to local file reads/search per the docs.
- Use filters (project, file path) where available to narrow results and speed searches.

Below is a complete, step‑by‑step trace of an MCP request starting in Claude Desktop and ending with a database query + response. I reference the relevant components / files from the repository so you can map each step to code.

High-level summary
- Claude Desktop launches the local MCP client (snipara-mcp).
- The client sends an MCP call to the Snipara MCP server (HTTP or stdio transport).
- The FastAPI server authenticates the request, checks usage/rate limits, loads project docs from the database (Prisma), runs the requested RLM tool, logs usage, and returns the result.
- The client receives the response and Claude Desktop displays it to the user.

Full flow (detailed)

1) User action in Claude Desktop
- The user composes a prompt and invokes the configured MCP server.
- Claude Desktop uses its config entry (claude_desktop_config.json) for the "snipara" MCP server:
  - command: "uvx"
  - args: ["snipara-mcp"]
  - env: SNIPARA_API_KEY, SNIPARA_PROJECT_ID
- Effect: Claude Desktop launches the snipara-mcp client process locally with those env vars.

2) Local MCP client (snipara-mcp) starts and prepares the MCP request
- snipara-mcp is the client package (apps/mcp-server/snipara-mcp/ and PyPI package). It either:
  - Runs as a client that issues an HTTP request to the hosted MCP server (common), or
  - Can act as an stdio MCP transport if configured (MCP supports stdio and HTTP transports).
- The client formulates the MCP request payload (JSON-RPC-like body or { tool, params }) and sets authentication headers:
  - API Key header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - OAuth header (if used): Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- Note: snipara-mcp may also consult ~/.snipara/tokens.json for an OAuth token before falling back to the env variable API key.

3) Transport to the MCP server
- If HTTP transport: client POSTs to server endpoint, e.g.:
  - POST /v1/{project_id}/mcp
  - Content-Type: application/json
  - Headers: X-API-Key or Authorization (see above)
- If stdio transport: the client and server exchange MCP messages over stdin/stdout (rlm-style).
- The server URL and header formats are defined in client config (.mcp.json or client env).

4) FastAPI MCP server receives the request (apps/mcp-server/src/server.py)
- Endpoint handler: POST /v1/{project_id}/mcp (server.py)
- server.py delegates authentication and request routing to the auth and transport layers.

5) Authentication & token selection (apps/mcp-server/src/auth.py)
- The server checks for:
  - OAuth token (Authorization bearer snipara_at_...) first (also checks stored token locations if used)
  - Otherwise checks X-API-Key header (API Key)
- API key validation is implemented in auth.py (SHA-256 hash comparison against DB/stored hashes).
- On invalid/missing credentials the server returns an auth error (invalid key/token).

6) Rate limiting & usage checks (apps/mcp-server/src/usage.py and config.py)
- Before executing the tool, server applies rate limits and plan-based monthly usage limits:
  - Default example: 100 requests/min per API key (Redis counters used)
  - Monthly quotas are checked (Query table / usage tracking)
- If over quota or rate-limited the server returns an error response (rate-limit / plan limit).

7) Load project configuration & documents (db.py + models.py)
- After auth + usage pass, server loads project metadata and documents:
  - Database access done via async Prisma client (apps/mcp-server/src/db.py; prisma/schema.prisma mirrors main schema).
  - Models and request/response shapes are defined in models.py (Pydantic).
- Typical loads:
  - Project record (project_id)
  - Project documents / knowledge base that RLM tools will use
  - API key / plan record (for usage attribution)

8) Initialize the RLM engine and select requested tool (rlm_engine.py)
- The server constructs and initializes the RLM engine (rlm_engine.py).
- The incoming request specifies which MCP tool to run (e.g., rlm_context_query, read, search, execute_python).
- rlm_engine maps the tool name to implementation and sets token budgets, plan limits, and any session context.

9) Tool execution: context retrieval, search, and DB reads
- Typical RLM tool steps:
  - If the tool is context_query: use internal search/index to find relevant doc sections (may call services that read saved docs from DB or a vector store).
  - If the tool needs to read files/line ranges, the engine calls read/sections/search helpers.
  - Some tools may spawn a REPL (execute_python) to run sandboxed code (Local REPL or DockerREPL) — REPL executes on server or in a sandbox and returns only small JSON results to the model.
- During tool execution the engine may issue additional DB reads (documents, embeddings, metadata) via db.py.
- The RLM stack enforces token budgets and may decompose complex queries into sub-queries (rlm_decompose, rlm_multi_query).

10) Logging and usage recording (usage.py / services / Query table)
- The server logs the query run details and resource usage:
  - Writes a row to Query table (timestamp, project_id, tool, tokens_used, success/failure)
  - Usage counters updated (monthly usage)
  - Rate limit counters incremented (Redis)
- Trajectory or debug logs (e.g., rlm runtime) may be recorded in JSONL for audit.

11) Formulate the MCP response (server.py / models.py)
- After tool finishes, server creates response JSON with fields like:
  - success: true/false
  - result: tool-specific payload (compact JSON or text)
  - error: error details if any
  - usage: tokens used, usage counters
- If an error occurred during tool execution, server returns an error structure and logs the trace.

12) Transport response back to client
- HTTP response returned to client (200 for success, 4xx/5xx for errors).
- For stdio transports the server writes the response back over stdout.

13) Local client receives and passes result to Claude Desktop
- snipara-mcp client maps MCP response to the Claude Desktop MCP format.
- If this was an interactive session, client may inject first-query tool tips, session context, or subsequent prompts per client logic.
- The client returns the result to Claude Desktop which renders it to the user.

14) Post-request hooks and persistence
- PostToolUse hooks (if configured) may run — e.g., record accessed files, update session state, call rlm_inject.
- Session context may be preserved in the DB or a session store for follow-up queries.

Files / components to inspect (where behavior is implemented)
- Client side:
  - apps/mcp-server/snipara-mcp/ (client implementation, CLI)
  - Claude Desktop config: claude_desktop_config.json (points to snipara-mcp)

Common error paths and responses

1) Client configuration / request origin (Claude Desktop or other MCP client)
- Claude Desktop can be configured to talk to an MCP server. Example (macOS) snippet added to claude_desktop_config.json:

      }
    }
- Transport options the client may use:
  - HTTP: POST to server URL (typical hosted deployment)
  - stdio: run a local binary (uvx/rlm) acting as MCP server

2) Client builds MCP request
- Endpoint: POST /v1/{project_id}/mcp
- Required header: X-API-Key (or Authorization in some clients)
- Body: JSON such as { "tool": "context_query", "params": { ... } } or a JSON-RPC style payload for tools/list, etc.
- Example simplified request:
  POST /v1/snipara/mcp
  Headers: X-API-Key: rlm_xxx
  Body: { "tool": "context_query", "params": { "query": "auth flow", "max_tokens": 3000 } }

3) FastAPI MCP server receives request (apps/mcp-server/src/server.py)
- Main endpoint handler: async def mcp_endpoint(project_id, request: MCPRequest, x_api_key: Header(...))
- Timing starts (latency measurement)
- Request timeout is enforced when executing the RLM engine (REQUEST_TIMEOUT = 30s in code sample)

4) API key validation (apps/mcp-server/src/auth.py)
- validate_api_key(x_api_key, project_id)
- Validation uses SHA-256 hash comparison against stored API keys in DB (or another secure store)
- If invalid → HTTP 401 / error returned to client

5) Usage & rate-limit checks (apps/mcp-server/src/usage.py + Redis)
- call check_limits(project_id)
  - Checks plan-based monthly quotas
  - Rate limiting implemented (100 req/min per API key typical)
  - Uses Redis for counters
- If limit exceeded → HTTP 429 returned

6) Initialize RLM engine (apps/mcp-server/src/rlm_engine.py)
- server constructs RLMEngine(project_id)
- RLMEngine loads project configuration and relevant resources:
  - settings from config.py (Pydantic)
  - session/context persistence (session store)
  - may load team shared_context (if enabled)

7) RLM engine executes requested tool
- engine.execute(request.tool, request.params) is awaited (wrapped in asyncio.wait_for to enforce timeout)
- Tools executed by RLMEngine include (examples):
  - context_query — primary optimized context retrieval (calls internal search, relevance, chunking)
  - sections — list docs/sections available
  - search / read — full text/regex search and line-range reads
  - execute_python / REPL tools — sandboxed code execution (Local or Docker REPL)
  - rlm_shared_context — retrieve team standards if applicable
- Internal flow inside a context_query:
  - Load project documents from DB (db.py / Prisma client)
  - Run semantic retrieval / hybrid search across sections
  - Possibly call the REPL or other tools for lightweight computation
  - Assemble a compact response (model-facing context or final answer)
- RLM engine returns a structured result object with:
  - data (tool output)
  - telemetry: total_tokens, search_time_ms, sections_scanned, etc.

8) Database access (apps/mcp-server/src/db.py + prisma/schema.prisma)
- The engine uses an async Prisma client to query the project DB (Postgres) for:
  - Project documents and sections
  - API key records (for validate_api_key)
  - Usage/Query records
- Typical DB queries:
  - SELECT project documents by project_id
  - SELECT/UPDATE usage counters, insert Query row with metadata

9) Usage tracking & telemetry (apps/mcp-server/src/usage.py)
- After successful execution, server calls track_usage(...):
  - track_usage(project_id, tool, output_tokens, latency_ms, success=True)
  - This inserts/updates a usage record (month quotas), logs Query rows
- If exception or timeout occurs, error branch still calls track_usage with success=False and error details

10) Response formation & return (server.py)
- On success:
  - Build MCPResponse with fields:
    success: true
    result: result.data
    usage: { output_tokens, latency_ms, search_time_ms, sections_scanned }
- On timeout:
  - success: false
  - error: "Request timeout (30s)"
  - usage: {"latency_ms": 30000}
- On other exceptions:
  - success: false
  - error: str(e)
  - usage includes latency
- Response returned as JSON to the client over HTTP (or written to stdio for stdio transport)

11) Client receives response (Claude Desktop)
- Claude Desktop (or other client) receives the MCPResponse JSON and uses the result to continue the user interaction (inject into LLM prompt, display to user, etc.)
- If first query in a session, server-side tools may include “first query tips” that are automatically injected (~300–500 tokens) depending on plan

12) Post-request bookkeeping & telemetry
- Server logs the Request/Response + telemetry (trajectory logging for RLM runtime if enabled)
- Rate-limiter counters are updated (Redis)
- Optionally, the engine may persist session context for subsequent requests

Error handling & security notes
- API key validation uses hashed comparison (auth.py)
- Rate limiting and monthly quotas enforced via Redis/usage module
- Request execution timeout protects server (REQUEST_TIMEOUT = 30s by default)
- REPL execution runs in restricted sandboxes (Local RestrictedPython or DockerREPL with network disabled by default)
- Server health endpoint: GET /health
- Pre-commit checks must be run locally before commits: pnpm lint, pnpm type-check, SKIP_ENV_VALIDATION=true pnpm build

1) Claude Desktop launches the MCP client
- Claude Desktop config (example in docs) runs a local MCP client instead of directly calling HTTP:

- This causes Claude Desktop to start the snipara-mcp process (package installed via pip / uvx). The local client (snipara-mcp) acts as the bridge between Claude and the hosted MCP server.

2) snipara-mcp builds & sends the MCP request to the hosted server
- The client issues an HTTP POST to the server endpoint for the project. (Docs show the server endpoint layout; the server code exposes POST /{project_id} in mcp_transport.py and the monorepo describes POST /v1/{project_id}/mcp.)
- Typical headers (priority order supported by server):
  - X-API-Key: rlm_...   (API key flow)
  - Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...  (OAuth flow)
- Typical JSON-RPC-like request body (example):
  {
    "jsonrpc": "2.0",
    "id": 1,
    "method": "tools/context_query",
    "params": { "query": "explain auth flow", "max_tokens": 3000 }
  }
- The client may obtain the X-API-Key from the environment (SNIPARA_API_KEY) or from .mcp.json config which can set headers.

3) Transport + authentication: mcp_transport.py → auth.py
- FastAPI endpoint receives the HTTP POST (router.post("/{project_id}") in src/mcp_transport.py).
- Headers extracted: X-API-Key (alias header), Authorization (Bearer token). The endpoint code checks headers and returns proper error messages when missing/invalid keys (see mcp_transport.py snippets).
- auth.py validates credentials:
  - API keys validated (SHA-256 hash comparison against stored hashes).
  - OAuth tokens are checked (priority: stored OAuth token in ~/.snipara/tokens.json; fallback to API key if OAuth not present).
- If auth fails, the transport returns an authentication error (HTTP 401/403 + JSON error).

4) Rate limiting & usage checks: usage.py + config.py
- Before executing the tool, the server enforces rate limits:
  - Per docs: 100 requests/min per API key (Redis-backed counters).
  - Monthly plan limits are enforced (config.py / plan limits).
- usage.py consults Redis for short-term rate limiting and queries the database for monthly usage counters when required.
- If limit exceeded, the server returns an appropriate error (429 / usage limit).

5) Load project data and session context: db.py + models.py
- The server loads project metadata, documents and any persisted session context from the database using the async Prisma client in db.py.
- Project-specific settings (tool availability, token budgets, plan) are read from DB or config.
- If Team/Shared context features are enabled, server may fetch team shared_context entries (rlm_shared_context support).

6) Initialize RLM engine and prepare execution: rlm_engine.py
- The server initializes the RLM engine (src/rlm_engine.py) with:
  - Token budgets, tool whitelist (based on plan)
  - Session id, user/project id, and any persisted context
  - If this is the first query in a session, the engine may auto-inject first-query tool tips into the context.
- The engine receives the requested method (tool) and parameters and plans execution (it may decompose the query, allocate budgets, and choose which sub-tools to call).

7) Execute the requested tool(s)
- Tools are implemented either in rlm_engine or via the Snipara tool registry. Typical tool types and where they execute:
  - rlm_context_query / search / sections / read → query the documentation index + possibly DB (internal doc store) and return optimized context sections. The engine may use semantic search.
  - rlm_shared_context → fetch team standards (if Team plan).
  - execute_code / REPL → run sandboxed Python (local REPL or DockerREPL) if the tool needs to compute something. REPLs are used so the LLM need not simulate computation.
  - If a tool needs structured data (project metadata, user info, saved snippets), the RLM engine calls db.py (Prisma client) to run async database queries.
- DB access example: rlm_engine needs to find documents for the project → calls db.py → Prisma executes SELECT queries against Document/Project tables and returns results.
- Any sensitive data access is controlled by auth + plan policies.

8) Record usage & persist query
- After execution the server records the query/response in the DB for analytics, billing and session persistence (Query table or similar).
- usage.py increments counters (monthly usage, request counters). Rate limit windows and usage quotas get updated (Redis + DB as described).

9) Construct and send the response
- The server packages the execution result in the response format documented in 3.1:
  - { "success": true, "result": <tool result>, "error": null, "usage": { ... } }
- If the client uses JSON-RPC, the server returns the JSON-RPC result structure with id and result fields.
- HTTP status typically 200 for success; errors are returned with proper HTTP status and error body.

10) snipara-mcp client receives response → Claude Desktop
- The client receives the HTTP response and forwards the tool result back to Claude Desktop (the Claude UI / runtime receives the result and displays it to the user).
- If the requested tool produced a short JSON payload (e.g., REPL returned a JSON summary), that small result is what is delivered to the LLM UI — which keeps token usage low.

Failure modes, logging and extra protections
- Auth fails → immediate 401/403 from mcp_transport.py (auth.py).
- Rate limit exceeded → 429 from usage.py.
- Tool runtime errors: rlm_engine catches and returns error stack with limited debugging info; trajectory logging (rlm-runtime) may record traces for debugging.
- DB failures → server returns 5xx and logs Prisma error; retry/backoff can be applied client-side.
- Security: DockerREPL defaults to no outbound network, read-only mounts and CPU/memory/time limits for sandboxed execution.

- CLI/device flow: users obtain OAuth tokens via the device flow and the CLI stores tokens in ~/.snipara/tokens.json by default

Key formats and server handling
- API keys: prefix rlm_, server hashes the key with SHA‑256 and looks up the hash in the ApiKey table (project keys checked first, then team keys). Only the first 8 chars shown in logs/UI.
- OAuth access tokens: prefix snipara_at_, validated by DB lookup + expiry check. Refresh tokens have prefix snipara_rt_ and can be used to get new access tokens.

Security differences and tradeoffs
- OAuth (snipara_at_ / device flow)
  - Pros:
    - User-scoped auth with explicit tokens and scopes (read/write enforcement).
    - Tokens are revocable from dashboard or via token revoke endpoints (GET/DELETE /api/settings/tokens).
    - Designed for interactive sign-in (device flow) so you avoid pasting long secrets into CLIs.
    - Supports refresh tokens to rotate short-lived access tokens.
  - Cons / notes:
    - Access tokens may still be long-lived depending on configuration; refresh tokens must be protected.
    - CLI stores tokens locally (e.g., ~/.snipara/tokens.json) — that file becomes a sensitive artifact.
- API keys (rlm_)
  - Pros:
    - Simple to use for machine-to-machine calls (Inject API, MCP).
    - Server stores only hashes (SHA‑256) of keys, reducing risk if DB is leaked.
    - Can be scoped by project (project key) or broader (team key).
  - Cons / notes:
    - Static credentials: if leaked they grant immediate access until rotated/revoked.
    - Team keys are broader in scope (access all team projects) — higher blast radius than project keys.
    - Must be rotated and stored securely; embedding in code or repos is risky.
- Stored CLI tokens vs environment keys
  - CLI prefers stored OAuth tokens (tokens.json) first, then environment API key. That means a compromised machine with a tokens.json or environment variable can be abused.
  - You can remove tokens.json to force API key usage (rm ~/.snipara/tokens.json).

Practical security recommendations
- Use the least-privilege credential: prefer project API keys over team keys for automations.
- Prefer OAuth (device flow) for interactive user auth and when you need per-user scopes and easy revocation.
- Protect credential storage: keep keys/tokens out of source control and use secrets managers or environment variables with restricted access.
- Rotate and revoke keys/tokens regularly; use the dashboard or the tokens API to revoke OAuth tokens.
- For automation or CI, use project API keys stored securely (secret manager) rather than personal OAuth tokens.

Short answer — available options:

3. OAuth access tokens (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...) issued via the OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628), with refresh tokens (snipara_rt_...)

Where each is accepted
- MCP server (api.snipara.com/mcp/{slug}) accepts either X-API-Key or Authorization: Bearer.
- Inject API (www.snipara.com/api/projects/{slug}/automation/inject) accepts only X-API-Key.

How they differ in security (details)

API keys (rlm_...)
- Format/storage: keys have an rlm_ prefix and the server stores only a SHA‑256 hash (keyHash) in DB; logs/UI show only first 8 chars.
- Lifetime: typically long‑lived until revoked (no expiry noted in docs). Revocation must be done via the dashboard or API.
- Scope: there are project keys (project-limited) and team keys (work across all team projects). Team keys have a larger blast radius.
- Risk profile: if leaked, an API key acts as a bearer credential and grants access until revoked — high risk for long-lived keys. Because Inject API requires X-API-Key, leaked key can be used against that endpoint too.
- Use case: automation, CI/CD, scripts — where non-interactive auth is needed.
- Best practices: use project keys for least privilege, store in environment/secret manager, rotate and revoke when compromised.

OAuth tokens (snipara_at_..., snipara_rt_...)
- Format/validation: access tokens use the snipara_at_ prefix and are validated by DB lookup + expiry check.
- Lifetime: access tokens expire; refresh tokens are used to obtain new access tokens (token refresh endpoint exists).
- Revocation: tokens can be listed (/api/settings/tokens) and revoked (DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId]).
- Security advantages:
  - Shorter-lived (limits exposure if leaked).
  - Can be tied to a user session and session metadata.
  - Device Flow avoids copy/pasting API keys: user authorizes in browser and CLI polls for token — better for interactive user auth on shared machines.
- Risk profile: local token storage (~/.snipara/tokens.json) is a single point — compromise of that file yields OAuth access until token expiry/revocation. OAuth tokens are still bearer tokens, but expiry + revocation + refresh lifecycle reduce long-term exposure vs. permanent API keys.
- Use case: interactive CLI usage, user-level access, per-user auditing.

Other operational notes / controls
- Authentication precedence for MCP: client checks for stored OAuth token (~/.snipara/tokens.json) first; if missing/invalid it falls back to API key (e.g., from env). You can remove ~/.snipara/tokens.json (or mv to a .bak) to force API-key flow.
  - Example: rm ~/.snipara/tokens.json
- Testing endpoints:
  - MCP test with API key:
    curl -X POST "https://api.snipara.com/mcp/snipara" -H "X-API-Key: rlm_your_key" -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/list"}'
  - Inject API requires X-API-Key similarly.
- Token refresh:
  - POST /api/oauth/token with grant_type=refresh_token to get a new access token.
- Error handling:
  - Expired/invalid OAuth token returns 401 with a message to re-authenticate.

Quick security recommendations
- For automation: prefer project-scoped API keys (least privilege), store them in secret managers, rotate regularly, and revoke compromised keys.
- For interactive CLI usage: prefer OAuth device flow (shorter-lived tokens, easier revocation and per-user audit).
- Avoid storing keys/tokens in code or public repos; remove local token files on multi-user hosts if you must force key-based auth.
- Use team keys only when you need cross-project access and accept the wider blast radius.

  - Project API key: limited to one project → lower blast radius (recommended for single-project automation).

- Lifetimes & rotation
  - API keys are typically long-lived until revoked/rotated by dashboard (shown once on creation).

  - API keys: server stores hashes (SHA‑256), reducing risk if DB dumps leak raw key values.
  - OAuth: server validates token and expiry; refresh tokens are sensitive and must be protected.
- Revocation & audit
  - OAuth: standard token listing/revocation endpoints; easier per-token lifecycle management.

  - OAuth device flow: avoids copying long keys, better for interactive user auth and per-user auditing.

  - Both use HTTPS; both are bearer tokens (if leaked, can be used by attacker).

  - Client/CLI checks stored OAuth token (~/.snipara/tokens.json) first, then falls back to API key from environment; removing tokens.json forces API-key authentication.

Practical recommendations
- Use OAuth device flow for interactive user sessions (shorter lifetimes, scopes, per-user audit).
- Use project-scoped API keys for automation limited to a single project.

Short answer
- When you run rlm_context_query, the total token budget is split between shared context (team collections) and project docs. Shared context is loaded first and apportioned by document category (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE). Project docs get the remaining budget.
- Within each bucket (project or a shared category) documents are sorted by relevance and included until that bucket’s token budget is exhausted.
- You can customize the split (e.g., shared_context_budget_percent) and the system enforces collection/token limits.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Query order
   - Shared context is consulted first, then project documents are queried with whatever token budget remains. This ensures team rules (especially MANDATORY) are considered before project-specific docs.

2. Overall split: shared vs project
   - The system reserves part of the total token budget for shared context and the rest for project documents. Higher‑level docs often describe this split as 40% shared / 60% project (i.e., project gets priority). The rlm_context_query parameter shared_context_budget_percent lets you override the shared/context split for a query.

3. Shared context category allocation
   - Shared context is further divided by document category. The intended category distribution (used in docs and examples) is:
     - MANDATORY — 40%
     - BEST_PRACTICES — 30%
     - GUIDELINES — 20%
     - REFERENCE — 10%
   - (These percentages describe how the shared-context portion is typically partitioned among categories.)

4. Implementation details (allocation algorithm)
   - The code groups docs into: project docs and shared docs by category.
   - A numeric budget is computed for each group (projectBudget and category budgets).
   - allocateWithinBudget sorts docs by relevanceScore (if present) and then adds documents in order until the group budget is used; docs that don’t fit are marked as not allocated (“exceeded budget”).
   - You can pass a customAllocation to override the default allocation mapping.

5. Default allocation values in the codebase
   - The token-budget implementation includes a DEFAULT_ALLOCATION mapping (source: apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts):
     - project: 0.40
     - MANDATORY: 0.25
     - BEST_PRACTICES: 0.20
     - GUIDELINES: 0.10
     - REFERENCE: 0.05
   - Note: those values sum to 1.0 (project 40% + shared categories 60%). Higher-level docs and examples sometimes present a 60% project / 40% shared split and a 40/30/20/10 breakdown for shared categories. The system provides knobs (shared_context_budget_percent and customAllocation) so teams can set the split that matches their intended behavior. If you need the authoritative setting for your deployment, check the token-budget implementation or the rlm_context_query parameters for your environment.

6. Hard limits & safety

   - MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION: 100,000
   - MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION: 50
   - MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD: 0.2
   - If a collection exceeds MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, enforceCollectionLimits will truncate documents from that collection to fit the limit.

Customization & practical notes
- Use rlm_context_query include_shared_context and shared_context_budget_percent to change how much budget shared context gets for a specific query.

Short answer: Snipara doesn’t yet document a one-click “collaboration” toggle in the pre-fetched docs. Use Snipara’s existing integrations (MCP/SSE, VS Code extension, and the API for uploading/indexing documents) plus a standard CRDT-based editing stack (recommended: Yjs + WebSocket transport + a persistence layer) to add real‑time collaborative editing. Below is a concise, practical plan with recommended architecture, security, persistence, and example code/config you can adapt.

1) Recommended architecture (production-ready)
- CRDT engine: Yjs (recommended) or Automerge — Yjs is lower-latency and has mature server impls.
- Transport: WebSocket (full duplex). SSE is available in Snipara for MCP flows (see Continue integration), but SSE is uni‑directional and not ideal for two‑way collaboration.
- Sync server: y-websocket (simple) or a custom WebSocket server that speaks the y-protocol.
- Presence/awareness: Yjs Awareness protocol (shows cursors, names).
- Persistence: snapshots + update log stored in Redis, Postgres, or object storage. Optionally pull compacted snapshots into Snipara via rlm_upload_document when you want them indexed.
- Indexing/Docs: When a collaborative doc is “saved” or periodically (e.g., every N minutes), call Snipara’s document upload/index API (rlm_upload_document or equivalent) so the collaborative edits are available to context queries.
- Access control & auth: JWT session tokens (short-lived) validated by the WS server. Use project-scoped API keys for server-side tasks only.
- Audit/history: Keep append-only update logs or store periodic snapshots for playback and auditing.

2) End-to-end steps to configure
A. Provision server(s)
- Run a y-websocket server (or custom) behind a load balancer.
- Add an auth middleware that validates JWTs (user identity + doc id + permissions).
- Add persistence: on document close or at intervals persist Yjs document snapshots/updates to Redis/Postgres/S3.

B. Client integration (browser or editor)
- Add Yjs dependency and WebSocket provider.
- Add Awareness to share cursors/names/colors.
- When document is considered “stable” (manual save or heartbeat), call Snipara index API to upload the latest content so it’s searchable.

C. Hook into Snipara
- Use the Snipara MCP/SSE if you need server → Snipara streaming for session context or to feed events (note: SSE is unidirectional).
- Use rlm_upload_document (or the Snipara documents API) to push snapshots or the canonical text for indexing.
- Optionally call rlm_remember to store ephemeral session context/decisions.

D. VS Code / other editor support
- For the Snipara VS Code extension, augment the extension to join the same CRDT session (the extension already provides Ask/Search/Inject context UI). Use the same auth token + WebSocket provider inside the extension to enable real-time editing/cursors.

3) Example pieces

A. Minimal browser client (Yjs + y-websocket)
- Install: npm i yjs y-websocket y-protocols
- Client snippet:
  const Y = require('yjs')
  const { WebsocketProvider } = require('y-websocket')
  const { Awareness } = require('y-protocols/awareness')

  const doc = new Y.Doc()
  // WebSocket URL protected by JWT:
  const wsUrl = 'wss://your-collab.example.com' + '/yjs?docId=DOC_ID'
  const token = '<JWT>'
  const provider = new WebsocketProvider(wsUrl, 'DOC_ID', doc, {
    params: { token } // or send token in Sec-WebSocket-Protocol or header via custom server
  })
  const awareness = provider.awareness
  // Bind a text editor (e.g., CodeMirror, ProseMirror) to Y.Text
  const ytext = doc.getText('quill') // or 'codemirror'
  // Awareness updates for cursors:
  awareness.setLocalStateField('user', { name: 'Alice', color: '#f00' })

B. y-websocket server with auth middleware (Node example)
- Use the y-websocket implementation and add a token check before accepting:
  const WebSocket = require('ws')
  const setupWSConnection = require('y-websocket/bin/utils.js').setupWSConnection
  const wss = new WebSocket.Server({ noServer: true })
  server.on('upgrade', (request, socket, head) => {
    // parse token from query or protocol header
    const token = parseToken(request)
    if (!validateToken(token, request)) {
      socket.write('HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized\r\n\r\n')
      socket.destroy()
      return
    }
    wss.handleUpgrade(request, socket, head, (ws) => {
      setupWSConnection(ws, request)
    })
  })

C. Persisting snapshots/updates
- Option A: subscribe to Yjs updates on the server and append them to a Redis stream or write to S3 periodically.
- Option B: on every N seconds or on save, call Snipara indexing API with the plain text snapshot:
  rlm_upload_document(path=`collab/${docId}.md`, content=docSnapshot, metadata={docId, lastEditedBy})

D. Integrating with Snipara MCP/SSE (when you need server → Snipara events)
- The docs show an MCP SSE transport block that uses X-API-Key. You can stream session events to the MCP SSE endpoint as server-side events for analytics or to feed session context into an RLM session:
  POST events → https://api.rlmsaas.com/v1/PROJECT_ID/mcp/sse  (use X-API-Key header)
- Note: for two‑way real-time editing use WebSocket for the CRDT; use MCP/SSE for sending session telemetry / non-real-time context to Snipara.

4) Security & operational recommendations
- Use short-lived JWTs for clients; validate doc-level permissions on WS connection.
- Rate limit and limit maximum doc size per connection.
- For large teams, shard by document namespace and use sticky sessions or a pub/sub layer (Redis pub/sub) so multiple WS servers can coordinate.
- Snapshot regularly and compact updates to avoid unbounded growth.
- Provide role-based access (read/write/comment) and make “upload to Snipara” a privilege or automated controlled action.
- Log CRDT update meta (who, when) for audit.

5) Testing & rollout checklist
- Unit tests: join/leave, concurrent edits, reconnect/resync.
- Load test: simulate many cursors and edits.
- Conflict/resync test: disconnect client, make edits elsewhere, reconnect.
- Integration: verify that rlm_upload_document produces the expected indexing output and that rlm_context_query returns the updated content within expected time.
